summary: Gruber examines the debate over the iPhone's software keyboard versus BlackBerry's physical keyboard, prompted by a CrackBerry 'Top 10' list he finds largely unconvincing. He surveyed his Twitter followers who switched from physical-keyboard phones to the iPhone and found the consensus is that it takes about a week to learn and a month to master, but most end up satisfied. He identifies the core marketing challenge: convincing skeptical physical-keyboard users to take a leap of faith on something that feels worse in a brief store demo. Ultimately, he argues this pocket of resistance is relatively insignificant to Apple's mass-market ambitions, since most future iPhone buyers are coming from basic phones with numeric keypads. However, he notes Apple clearly covets BlackBerry's enterprise customers and acknowledges the keyboard remains RIM's strongest card with that audience.
key_points: Twitter survey of iPhone switchers from physical keyboards shows most are happy after a learning curve of about a week to a month
The iPhone keyboard's weakness is its trial experience — a few minutes in a store won't convince a skeptical BlackBerry user, so adoption requires a leap of faith
People who regularly use both keyboard types tend to like the iPhone keyboard least, suggesting full commitment is required to build the necessary muscle memory
The physical-keyboard smartphone market is tiny compared to Apple's iPod-scale ambitions — most future iPhone buyers are coming from basic numeric keypads
Apple's enterprise push with iPhone 2.0 shows it covets BlackBerry switchers, but Gruber doubts Apple will ever release a physical-keyboard iPhone
money_quotes: My own opinion is simply that the iPhone keyboard works a lot better than I expected it to.
I suspect the only way for someone accustomed to a BlackBerry-style keyboard to get used to the iPhone keyboard is to switch full-time. The necessary muscle memory is too different.
"It takes a couple of weeks to get used to it" means you've got to take it on faith.
Even if there's not a single already-using-a-smartphone user left who is going to switch to an iPhone, it wouldn't prevent the iPhone from being a mass market success.
No one can argue that typing on the iPhone doesn't beat the pants off gimmicks like T9.
The keyboard is perhaps the single biggest advantage RIM has with these customers.

If they did, it'd be one of those Steve Jobs 'a year ago he said these things were crap and now he's telling us this one's awesome just because it's from Apple' moves that drive some people — bless their hearts — spittle-flying-out-of-their-mouths crazy.
key_insight: The iPhone keyboard's real obstacle isn't that it's bad — it's that its quality only becomes apparent after weeks of committed use, creating a leap-of-faith problem that brief store demos can't solve.
